'''Avogadro's number''', also called '''Avogadro's constant''' ('''''N''<sub>A</sub>'''), named after [[Amedeo Avogadro]], is the number of atoms in a [[Mole (unit)|mole]] of a monatomic element or the number of molecules in a mole of a compound. Avogadro's number is formally defined in the [[SI]] as the number of [[carbon-12]] atoms in 0.0120000 kg of unbound carbon-12 in its rest-energy electronic state. The accepted value  is 6.0221415{{e|23}} atoms/mole. In the nineteenth century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about 1/(6.023x10<sup>23</sup>) grams; they were trying to evaluate how many molecules of an [[ideal gas]] would fit in 1 cubic centimeter [http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html].  Carbon-12 was chosen as the reference substance over hydrogen because its atomic mass could be measured more accurately.

A [[mole (unit)|mole]] is defined in the SI as Avogadro's number of particles of any kind of substance ([[atom]]s, [[ion]]s, [[molecule]]s, or [[formula unit]]s). In the SI, this unit is abbreviated mol. The mole is the basic unit of [[amount of substance]].

==History==
'''Avogadro's number''' is named after the early nineteenth century Italian scientist [[Amedeo Avogadro]].
It appears that [[Jean Baptiste Perrin]] was the first to name it. Perrin called it "Avogadro's constant" and it is still sometimes known by that name. The numerical value was first estimated by [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[Kinetic theory of gases|kinetic gas theory]]. In German-speaking countries, the number may still be referred to as '''Loschmidt's number'''. Unfortunately, in a few cases (mainly in the older literature) '''[[Loschmidt's number]]''' refers to the number of atoms (or molecules) in a cubic centimeter, a usage now disparaged, viz: [http://gemini.tntech.edu/~tfurtsch/scihist/loschmid.html].

==Application==
Avogadro's number can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 [[atomic mass unit|u]], so Avogadro's number of iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains Avogadro's number of iron atoms. Thus Avogadro's number <math>N_{\rm A}</math> corresponds to the conversion factor between grams (g) and atomic mass units:

:<math>1\ \mbox{g}=N_{\rm A} \mbox{u}.</math>

==Chemical significance of Avogadro's number==
The value of Avogadro's number depends on the definition of the mole, which depends on the definition of the kilogram. Both definitions, especially that of the kilogram, are arbitrary: the kilogram system is currently based on the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. This means that the particular value of Avogadro's number is the result of convention; there is no physical reason for it. For this reason, Avogadro's number is not considered a [[fundamental physical constant|fundamental constant]] in the strictest sense. However, for practical purposes, Avogadro's number is regarded as a chemical constant.

'''Avogadro's number''' can be regarded as a conversion factor between the microscopic mass system ([[atomic mass unit]]s or Daltons) and the [[kilogram]] system. The microscopic mass system is based on the mass of carbon-12, while the kilogram system is currently based on the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. So naturally there's no simple conversion factor between the two. However, if a method were developed to count atoms, it would be possible to redefine the kilogram in a way that did not depend on an arbitrary cylinder of metal. The number of atoms picked would presumably be equal or close to the latest accepted value of Avogadro's number. In that case, the kilogram would be redefined as the mass of {{frac|1|0.012}} = 83{{frac|1|3}} times Avogadro's number of carbon-12 atoms.

==Additional physical relations==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, Avogadro's number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between an atomic mass scale and a kilogram ([[SI]]) scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[universal gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>: [[Universal gas constant|''R'']] = [[Boltzmann constant|''k'']]<sub>B</sub> ''N''<sub>A</sub>
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] e: [[Faraday constant|''F'']] = [[Elementary charge|e]] ''N''<sub>A</sub>

In the 19th century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about {{frac|1|6.02214199{{e|23}}}} grams. The gram was originally defined to be the mass of a cubic centimeter of pure water at standard temperature and pressure [http://www.factmonster.com/ce6/sci/A0832931.html]. As experiments became more accurate, it was found that water was contaminated with variable amounts of heavy water, which made it undesirable to maintain a standard with hydrogen having one atomic mass unit. Carbon was found to have a more constant isotopic composition, and it was also possible to separate pure carbon-12. Therefore, the atomic mass unit was changed to {{frac|1|12}} the mass of an atom of carbon-12. Hence 12 grams of carbon-12 has about 6.0221415{{e|23}} atoms. The recent history and more details can be found in the document, [http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1992/pdf/6410x1535.pdf Atomic Weight: The Name, Its History, Definition and Units].

== Numerical value ==
At present it is not technologically feasible to count the exact number of atoms in 12 g of carbon-12, so the precise value of Avogadro's number is unknown. The 2002 [[CODATA]] recommended value for Avogadro's number is 
:<math>N_{\rm A} = 6.022\,1415 \times 10^{23} \pm 0.000\,0010 \times 10^{23}  \mathrm{mol}^{-1}</math>.

A number of methods can be used to measure Avogadro's number. One modern method is to calculate Avogadro's number from the density of a crystal, the relative atomic mass, and the unit cell length determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for silicon have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of Avogadro's number.

Thus, protons and neutrons in the nucleus have masses that are less (about 0.7 percent less) than those of free protons and neutrons. The precise amount of mass loss is related to the binding energy of the nucleus and varies depending on the type of atom.

One may therefore say that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is approximately the number of ''nuclear'' neutrons or protons (nucleons) that have a mass of 1 gram. This is approximate because the precise mass of a nuclear proton or neutron depends on the composition of the nucleus, as explained above. For example, [[iron]] nucleons will have a significantly lower mass than those in [[hydrogen]] or [[plutonium]].

== Avogadro's number in life==
Avogadro's number may also yield practical reasonings in real life. For example, the fact that a known number of atoms are in a given amount of a substance is one reason for scientific criticism of [[homeopathy]], in which medicinal substances are often diluted to the extent that a single molecule appears in only one dose amongst the hundreds or thousands prepared, as a simple calculation involving Avogadro's number will reveal.

== History ==
'''Avogadro's number''' is named after the early nineteenth century Italian scientist [[Amedeo Avogadro]].
It appears that [[Jean Baptiste Perrin]] was the first to name it. Perrin called it "Avogadro's constant" and it is still sometimes known by that name. The numerical value was first estimated by [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[Kinetic theory of gases|kinetic gas theory]]. In German-speaking countries, the number may still be referred to as '''Loschmidt's number'''. Unfortunately, in a few cases (mainly in the older literature) '''[[Loschmidt's number]]''' refers to the number of atoms (or molecules) in a cubic centimeter, a usage now disparaged, viz: [http://gemini.tntech.edu/~tfurtsch/scihist/loschmid.html].

In the nineteenth century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about 1/(6.023x10<sup>23</sup>) grams; they were trying to evaluate how many molecules of an [[ideal gas]] would fit in 1 cubic centimeter [http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html] at [[Standard conditions for temperature and pressure|STP]] ... which is related to Avogadro's number via the ideal gas law.  Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions based 16 grams of oxygen : physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 ended this disupte and had other advantages [http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html].

== Application ==
Avogadro's number can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 [[atomic mass unit|u]], so Avogadro's number of iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains Avogadro's number of iron atoms. Thus Avogadro's number <math>N_{\rm A}</math> corresponds to the conversion factor between grams (g) and atomic mass units:

:<math>1\ \mbox{g}=N_{\rm A} \mbox{u}.</math>

== Chemical significance of Avogadro's number ==
The definition of Avogadro's number depends on the definition of the kilogram.  The latter is based on arbitrary convention, namely the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. This means that the particular value of Avogadro's number is the result of convention; there is no physical reason for it. For this reason, Avogadro's number is not considered a [[fundamental physical hat hat hat constant|fundamental constant]] in the strictest sense. However, for practical purposes, Avogadro's number is regarded as a chemical constant.

'''Avogadro's number''' can be regarded as a conversion factor between the microscopic mass system ([[atomic mass unit]]s or Daltons) and the [[kilogram]] system. The microscopic mass system is based on the mass of carbon-12, while the kilogram system is currently based on the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. So naturally there's no simple conversion factor between the two. However, if a method were developed to count atoms, it would be possible to redefine the kilogram in a way that did not depend on an arbitrary cylinder of metal. The number of atoms picked would presumably be equal or close to the latest accepted value of Avogadro's number. In that case, the kilogram would be redefined as the mass of {{frac|1|0.012}} = 83{{frac|1|3}} times Avogadro's number of carbon-12 atoms.

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, Avogadro's number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between an atomic mass scale and a kilogram ([[SI]]) scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[universal gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>: [[Universal gas constant|''R'']] = [[Boltzmann constant|''k'']]<sub>B</sub> ''N''<sub>A</sub>
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] e: [[Faraday constant|''F'']] = [[Elementary charge|e]] ''N''<sub>A</sub>

In the 19th century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about {{frac|1|6.02214199{{e|23}}}} grams. The gram was originally defined to be the mass of a cubic centimeter of pure water at standard temperature and pressure [http://www.factmonster.com/ce6/sci/A0832931.html]. As experiments became more accurate, it was found that water was contaminated with variable amounts of heavy water, which made it undesirable to maintain a standard with hydrogen having one atomic mass unit. Carbon was found to have a more constant isotopic composition, and it was also possible to separate pure carbon-12. Therefore, the atomic mass unit was changed to {{frac|1|12}} the mass of an atom of carbon-12. Hence 12 grams of carbon-12 has about 6.0221415{{e|23}} atoms. The recent history and more details can be found in the document, [http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1992/pdf/6410x1535.pdf Atomic Weight: The Name, Its History, Definition and Units].

== Numerical value ==
At present it is not technologically feasible to count the exact number of atoms in 12 g of carbon-12, so the precise value of Avogadro's number is unknown. The 2002 [[CODATA]] recommended value for Avogadro's number is 
:<math>N_{\rm A} = 6.022\,1415 \times 10^{23} \pm 0.000\,0010 \times 10^{23}  \mathrm{mol}^{-1}</math>.

A number of methods can be used to measure Avogadro's number. One modern method is to calculate Avogadro's number from the density of a crystal, the relative atomic mass, and the unit cell length determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for silicon have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of Avogadro's number.

== Connection to masses of protons and neutrons ==
A carbon-12 atom consists of 6 [[proton]]s and 6 [[neutron]]s (which have approximately the same mass) and 6 [[electron]]s (whose mass is negligible as a first approximation, being about {{frac|1|1840}} of the mass of the proton). One could therefore think that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is the number of protons or neutrons that have a mass of 1 gram. While this is approximately correct, the mass of a free proton is 1.00727 amu, so a mole of protons would actually have a mass of 1.00727 g. Similarly, a mole of neutrons has a mass of 1.00866 g. Clearly, 6 moles of protons combined with six moles of neutrons would have a mass greater than 12 g. So it would appear that one mole of carbon-12 atoms, which should consist of 6 moles each of protons, neutrons, and electrons would have a mass greater than 12 g. The discrepancy or [[mass defect]] (as it is known) is related to the [[equivalence of matter and energy]] discovered by [[Albert Einstein]] as part of the theory of [[special relativity]]. When an atom is formed, the protons and neutrons in the nucleus are bound together by the [[strong nuclear force]]. This binding results in the formation of a low energy state and is accompanied by a large release of energy. Since energy is equivalent to mass (which means that all energy ''has'' mass), the released energy has mass and carries away the loss in the mass of the nucleus relative to that of the separated protons and neutrons (note that mass is conserved in this process just as energy is). Thus, protons and neutrons in the nucleus have masses that are less (about 0.7 percent less) than those of free protons and neutrons. The precise amount of mass loss is related to the binding energy of the nucleus and varies depending on the type of atom.

One may therefore say that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is approximately the number of ''nuclear'' neutrons or protons (nucleons) that have a mass of 1 gram. This is approximate because the precise mass of a nuclear proton or neutron depends on the composition of the nucleus, as explained above. For example, [[iron]] nucleons will have a significantly lower mass than those in [[hydrogen]] or [[plutonium]].

== Avogadro's number in life ==
Avogadro's number may also yield practical reasonings in real life. For example, the fact that a known number of atoms are in a given amount of a substance is one reason for scientific criticism of [[homeopathy]], in which medicinal substances are often diluted to the extent that a single molecule appears in only one dose amongst the hundreds or thousands prepared, as a simple calculation involving Avogadro's number will reveal.

Another common sense application shows that without determining the actual weight of a substance, a good rule of thumb to use is that a cubic centimeter of solid matter contains about 10<sup>24</sup> atoms [http://pages.prodigy.net/jhonig/bignum/qatoms.html].

So it would appear that one mole of carbon-12 atoms, which should consist of 6 moles each of protons, neutrons, and electrons would have a mass greater than 12 g. The discrepancy or [[mass defect]] (as it is known) is related to the [[equivalence of matter and energy]] discovered by [[Albert Einstein]] as part of the theory of [[special relativity]]. When an atom is formed, the protons and neutrons in the nucleus are bound together by the [[strong nuclear force]]. This binding results in the formation of a low energy state and is accompanied by a large release of energy. Since energy is equivalent to mass (which means that all energy ''has'' mass), the released energy has mass and carries away the loss in the mass of the nucleus relative to that of the separated protons and neutrons (note that mass is conserved in this process just as energy is). Thus, protons and neutrons in the nucleus have masses that are less (about 0.7 percent less) than those of free protons and neutrons. The precise amount of mass loss is related to the binding energy of the nucleus and varies depending on the type of atom.

One may therefore say that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is approximately the number of ''nuclear'' neutrons or protons (nucleons) that have a mass of 1 gram. This is approximate because the precise mass of a nuclear proton or neutron depends on the composition of the nucleus, as explained above. For example, [[iron]] nucleons will have a significantly lower mass than those in [[hydrogen]] or [[plutonium]].

== Avogadro's number in life ==
Avogadro's number may also yield practical reasonings in real life. For example, the fact that a known number of atoms are in a given amount of a substance is one reason for scientific criticism of [[homeopathy]], in which medicinal substances are often diluted to the extent that a single molecule appears in only one dose amongst the hundreds or thousands prepared, as a simple calculation involving Avogadro's number will reveal.

Another common sense application shows that without determining the actual weight of a substance, a good rule of thumb to use is that a cubic centimeter of solid matter contains about 10<sup>24</sup> atoms [http://pages.prodigy.net/jhonig/bignum/qatoms.html].

'''Avogadro's number''', also called '''Avogadro's constant''' ('''''N''<sub>A</sub>'''), named after [[Amedeo Avogadro]], in German scientific literature also known as '''[[Loschmidt number]]''', is formally defined to be the number of [[carbon-12]] atoms in 12 grams (0.012 kg) of unbound carbon-12 in its rest-energy electronic state. The current best estimate of this number is (6.0221415 ± 0.0000010){{e|23}}.

A [[mole (unit)|mole]] is defined in the SI as Avogadro's number of particles of any kind of substance ([[atom]]s, [[ion]]s, [[molecule]]s, or [[formula unit]]s). In the SI, this unit is abbreviated mol. The mole is widely used as a macroscopic unit of [[amount of substance]].

== History ==
'''Avogadro's number''' is named after the early nineteenth century Italian scientist [[Amedeo Avogadro]].
It appears that [[Jean Baptiste Perrin]] was the first to name it. Perrin called it "Avogadro's constant" and it is still sometimes known by that name. The numerical value was first estimated by [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[Kinetic theory of gases|kinetic gas theory]]. In German-speaking countries, the number may still be referred to as '''Loschmidt's number'''. Unfortunately, in a few cases (mainly in the older literature) '''[[Loschmidt's number]]''' refers to the number of atoms (or molecules) in a cubic centimeter, a usage now disparaged, viz: [http://gemini.tntech.edu/~tfurtsch/scihist/loschmid.html].

In the nineteenth century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about 1/(6.023x10<sup>23</sup>) grams; they were trying to evaluate how many molecules of an [[ideal gas]] would fit in 1 cubic centimeter [http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html] at [[Standard conditions for temperature and pressure|STP]] ... which is related to Avogadro's number via the ideal gas law.  Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions based 16 grams of oxygen : physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 ended this dispute and had other advantages [http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html].

== Application ==
Avogadro's number can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 [[atomic mass unit|u]], so Avogadro's number of iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains Avogadro's number of iron atoms. Thus Avogadro's number <math>N_{\rm A}</math> corresponds to the conversion factor between grams (g) and atomic mass units:

:<math>1\ \mbox{g}=N_{\rm A} \mbox{u}.</math>

== Chemical significance of Avogadro's number ==
The definition of Avogadro's number depends on the definition of the kilogram.  The latter is based on arbitrary convention, namely the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. This means that the particular value of Avogadro's number is the result of convention; there is no physical reason for it. For this reason, Avogadro's number is not considered a [[fundamental physical constant|fundamental constant]] in the strictest sense. However, for practical purposes, Avogadro's number is regarded as a chemical constant.

'''Avogadro's number''' can be regarded as a conversion factor between the microscopic mass system ([[atomic mass unit]]s or Daltons) and the [[kilogram]] system. The microscopic mass system is based on the mass of carbon-12, while the kilogram system is currently based on the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. So naturally there's no simple conversion factor between the two. However, if a method were developed to count atoms, it would be possible to redefine the kilogram in a way that did not depend on an arbitrary cylinder of metal. The number of atoms picked would presumably be equal or close to the latest accepted value of Avogadro's number. In that case, the kilogram would be redefined as 83.33 x the mass of Avogadro's number of carbon-12 atoms (i.e. 83.33 x 12 g = 1 Kg).

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, Avogadro's number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between an atomic mass scale and a kilogram ([[SI]]) scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[universal gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>: [[Universal gas constant|''R'']] = [[Boltzmann constant|''k'']]<sub>B</sub> ''N''<sub>A</sub>
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] e: [[Faraday constant|''F'']] = [[Elementary charge|e]] ''N''<sub>A</sub>

In the 19th century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about 6.02214199{{e|-23}} grams. The gram was originally defined to be the mass of a cubic centimeter of pure water at standard temperature and pressure [http://www.factmonster.com/ce6/sci/A0832931.html]. As experiments became more accurate, it was found that water was contaminated with variable amounts of heavy water, which made it undesirable to maintain a standard with hydrogen having one atomic mass unit. Carbon was found to have a more constant isotopic composition, and it was also possible to separate pure carbon-12. Therefore, the atomic mass unit was changed to {{frac|1|12}} the mass of an atom of carbon-12. Hence 12 grams of carbon-12 has about 6.0221415{{e|23}} atoms. The recent history and more details can be found in the document, [http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1992/pdf/6410x1535.pdf Atomic Weight: The Name, Its History, Definition and Units].

== Numerical value ==
At present it is not technologically feasible to count the exact number of atoms in 12 g of carbon-12, so the precise value of Avogadro's number is unknown. The 2002 [[CODATA]] recommended value for Avogadro's number is 
:<math>N_{\rm A} = 6.022\,1415 \times 10^{23} \pm 0.000\,0010 \times 10^{23}  \mathrm{mol}^{-1}</math>.

A number of methods can be used to measure Avogadro's number. One modern method is to calculate Avogadro's number from the density of a crystal, the relative atomic mass, and the unit cell length determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for silicon have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of Avogadro's number.

== Connection to masses of protons and neutrons ==
A carbon-12 atom consists of 6 [[proton]]s and 6 [[neutron]]s (which have approximately the same mass) and 6 [[electron]]s (whose mass is negligible as a first approximation, being about {{frac|1|1840}} of the mass of the proton). One could therefore think that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is the number of protons or neutrons that have a mass of 1 gram. While this is approximately correct, the mass of a free proton is 1.00727 amu, so a mole of protons would actually have a mass of 1.00727 g. Similarly, a mole of neutrons has a mass of 1.00866 g. Clearly, 6 moles of protons combined with six moles of neutrons would have a mass greater than 12 g. So it would appear that one mole of carbon-12 atoms, which should consist of 6 moles each of protons, neutrons, and electrons would have a mass greater than 12 g. The discrepancy or [[mass defect]] (as it is known) is related to the [[equivalence of matter and energy]] discovered by [[Albert Einstein]] as part of the theory of [[special relativity]]. When an atom is formed, the protons and neutrons in the nucleus are bound together by the [[strong nuclear force]]. This binding results in the formation of a low energy state and is accompanied by a large release of energy. Since energy is equivalent to mass (which means that all energy ''has'' mass), the released energy has mass and carries away the loss in the mass of the nucleus relative to that of the separated protons and neutrons (note that mass is conserved in this process just as energy is). Thus, protons and neutrons in the nucleus have masses that are less (about 0.7 percent less) than those of free protons and neutrons. The precise amount of mass loss is related to the binding energy of the nucleus and varies depending on the type of atom.

One may therefore say that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is approximately the number of ''nuclear'' neutrons or protons (nucleons) that have a mass of 1 gram. This is approximate because the precise mass of a nuclear proton or neutron depends on the composition of the nucleus, as explained above. For example, [[iron]] nucleons will have a significantly lower mass than those in [[hydrogen]] or [[plutonium]].

== Avogadro's number in life ==
Avogadro's number may also yield practical reasonings in real life. For example, the fact that a known number of atoms are in a given amount of a substance is one reason for scientific criticism of [[homeopathy]], in which medicinal substances are often diluted to the extent that a single molecule appears in only one dose amongst the hundreds or thousands prepared, as a simple calculation involving Avogadro's number will reveal.

Another common sense application shows that without determining the actual weight of a substance, a good rule of thumb to use is that a cubic centimeter of solid matter contains about 10<sup>24</sup> atoms [http://pages.prodigy.net/jhonig/bignum/qatoms.html].

In the nineteenth century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about 1/(6.023x10<sup>23</sup>) grams in an attempt to measure the number of [[ideal gas]] molecules would fit in 1 cubic centimeter [http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html] at [[Standard conditions for temperature and pressure|STP]] ... which is related to Avogadro's number via the [[ideal gas law]].  Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions based on 16 grams of oxygen : physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 ended this dispute and had other advantages [http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html].

== Application ==
Avogadro's number can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 [[atomic mass unit|u]], so Avogadro's number of iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains Avogadro's number of iron atoms. Thus Avogadro's number <math>N_{\rm A}</math> corresponds to the conversion factor between grams (g) and atomic mass units:

:<math>1\ \mbox{g}=N_{\rm A} \mbox{u}.</math>

== Chemical significance of Avogadro's number ==
Actually Avogadro's real first name is Sarah @ www.myspace.com/myknife__yourthroat! The definition of Avogadro's number depends on the definition of the kilogram.  The latter is based on arbitrary convention, namely the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. This means that the particular value of Avogadro's number is the result of convention; there is no physical reason for it. For this reason, Avogadro's number is not considered a [[fundamental physical constant|fundamental constant]] in the strictest sense. However, for practical purposes, Avogadro's number is regarded as a chemical constant.

'''Avogadro's number''' can be regarded as a conversion factor between the microscopic mass system ([[atomic mass unit]]s or Daltons) and the [[kilogram]] system. The microscopic mass system is based on the mass of carbon-12, while the kilogram system is currently based on the mass of a particular "standard" cylinder of metal in France. So naturally there's no simple conversion factor between the two. However, if a method were developed to count atoms, it would be possible to redefine the kilogram in a way that did not depend on an arbitrary cylinder of metal. The number of atoms picked would presumably be equal or close to the latest accepted value of Avogadro's number. In that case, the kilogram would be redefined as 83.33 x the mass of Avogadro's number of carbon-12 atoms (i.e. 83.33 x 12 g = 1 kg).

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, Avogadro's number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between an atomic mass scale and a kilogram ([[SI]]) scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[universal gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>: [[Universal gas constant|''R'']] = [[Boltzmann constant|''k'']]<sub>B</sub> ''N''<sub>A</sub>
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] e: [[Faraday constant|''F'']] = [[Elementary charge|e]] ''N''<sub>A</sub>

In the 19th century physicists measured the mass of one atom of hydrogen to be about 1.660539{{e|-24}} grams. The gram was originally defined to be the mass of a cubic centimeter of pure water at standard temperature and pressure [http://www.factmonster.com/ce6/sci/A0832931.html]. As experiments became more accurate, it was found that water was contaminated with variable amounts of heavy water, which made it undesirable to maintain a standard with hydrogen having one atomic mass unit. Carbon was found to have a more constant isotopic composition, and it was also possible to separate pure carbon-12. Therefore, the atomic mass unit was changed to {{frac|1|12}} the mass of an atom of carbon-12. Hence 12 grams of carbon-12 has about 6.0221415{{e|23}} atoms. The recent history and more details can be found in the document, [http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1992/pdf/6410x1535.pdf Atomic Weight: The Name, Its History, Definition and Units].

== Numerical value ==
At present it is not technologically feasible to count the exact number of atoms in 12 g of carbon-12, so the precise value of Avogadro's number is unknown. The 2002 [[CODATA]] recommended value for Avogadro's number is 
:<math>N_{\rm A} = 6.022\,1415 \times 10^{23} \pm 0.000\,0010 \times 10^{23}  \mathrm{mol}^{-1}</math>.

A number of methods can be used to measure Avogadro's number. One modern method is to calculate Avogadro's number from the density of a crystal, the relative atomic mass, and the unit cell length determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for silicon have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of Avogadro's number.

== Connection to masses of protons and neutrons ==
A carbon-12 atom consists of 6 [[proton]]s and 6 [[neutron]]s (which have approximately the same mass) and 6 [[electron]]s (whose mass is negligible as a first approximation, being about {{frac|1|1840}} of the mass of the proton). One could therefore think that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is the number of protons or neutrons that have a mass of 1 gram. While this is approximately correct, the mass of a free proton is 1.00727 amu, so a mole of protons would actually have a mass of 1.00727 g. Similarly, a mole of neutrons has a mass of 1.00866 g. Clearly, 6 moles of protons combined with six moles of neutrons would have a mass greater than 12 g. So it would appear that one mole of carbon-12 atoms, which should consist of 6 moles each of protons, neutrons, and electrons would have a mass greater than 12 g. The discrepancy or [[mass defect]] (as it is known) is related to the [[equivalence of matter and energy]] discovered by [[Albert Einstein]] as part of the theory of [[special relativity]]. When an atom is formed, the protons and neutrons in the nucleus are bound together by the [[strong nuclear force]]. This binding results in the formation of a low energy state and is accompanied by a large release of energy. Since energy is equivalent to mass, the released energy carries away the loss in the mass of the nucleus relative to that of the separated protons and neutrons. Thus, protons and neutrons in the nucleus have masses that are less (about 0.7 percent less) than those of free protons and neutrons. The precise amount of mass loss is related to the [[binding energy]] of the nucleus and varies depending on the type of atom.

One may therefore say that ''N''<sub>A</sub> is approximately the number of ''nuclear'' neutrons or protons (nucleons) that have a mass of 1 gram. This is approximate because the precise mass of a nuclear proton or neutron depends on the composition of the nucleus, as explained above. For example, [[iron]] nucleons will have a significantly lower mass than those in [[hydrogen]] or [[plutonium]].

== Avogadro's number in life ==
Avogadro's number may also yield practical reasonings in real life. For example, the fact that a known number of atoms are in a given amount of a substance is one reason for scientific criticism of [[homeopathy]], in which medicinal substances are often diluted to the extent that a single molecule appears in only one dose amongst the hundreds or thousands prepared, as a simple calculation involving Avogadro's number will reveal.

Another common sense application shows that without determining the actual weight of a substance, a good rule of thumb to use is that a cubic centimeter of solid matter contains about 10<sup>24</sup> atoms [http://pages.prodigy.net/jhonig/bignum/qatoms.html].

The '''Avogadro constant''' (symbols: ''L'', ''N''<sub>A</sub>), also called the '''Avogadro number''' and, in German scientific literature, sometimes also known as the [[Loschmidt constant|Loschmidt constant/number]], is formally defined to be the number of "entities" in one [[mole (unit)|mole]],<ref>{{cite book|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Physicochemical Symbols Terminology and Units|title=Quantities, Units and Symbols in Physical Chemistry (2nd Edition)|publisher=Blackwell Scientific Publications|location=Oxford|date=1993
|url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/books/gbook/green_book_2ed.pdf|id=ISBN 0-632-03583-8|accessdate=2006-12-28}} {{cite journal|title=Glossary of Terms in Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry (IUPAC-IFCC Recommendations 1996)| |url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1996/pdf/6804x0957.pdf|journal=[[Pure and Applied Chemistry|Pure Appl. Chem.]]|volume=68|pages=957–1000|year=1996|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry|coauthors=[[International Federation of Clinical Chemistry]] Committee on Quantities and Units|accesdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> that is the number of [[carbon-12]] [[atom]]s in 12 [[gram]]s (0.012 [[Kilogram|kg]]) of unbound carbon-12 in its rest-energy electronic state. The current best estimate of this number is (6.022 1415 ± 0.000 0010)×10<sup>23</sup> [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]].<ref>[[Committee on Data for Science and Technology|CODATA]] [http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?na|search_for=abbr_in! (2002)].</ref>

== History and terminology ==
The '''Avogadro constant''' is named after the early [[nineteenth century]] [[Italy|Italian]] scientist [[Amedeo Avogadro]], who is credited with being the first to realise that the volume of a gas (strictly, of an [[ideal gas]]) is proportional to the number of atoms or [[molecule]]s.
It appears that [[Jean Baptiste Perrin]] was the first to name it.

Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant: the numerical value was first estimated by the [[Austria]]n physicist [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[kinetic theory of gases]].<ref>{{cite journal|title=Joseph Loschmidt, Physicist and Chemist|last=Bader|first=Alfred|coauthors=Parker, Leonard|journal=Physics Today Online|issue=March 2001|url=http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html|accessdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> In German-speaking countries, the constant may still be referred to as the ''[[Loschmidt constant]]'' or ''Loschmidt's number'': however this name is more correctly reserved for the number of particles in a given volume of an [[ideal gas]] (symbol:''n''<sub>0</sub>):<ref>{{cite web|url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?n0|search_for=physchem_in!|title=Fundamental physical constants: Physico-chemical constants|accessdate=2006-12-28|last=[[National Institute of Standards and Technology]]|date=February 2006}}</ref>
::''n''<sub>0</sub> = ''RT''/''p'' = (2.686 7773 ± 0.000 0047)×10<sup>25</sup> m<sup>−3</sup> at 273.15 [[Kelvin (unit)|K]] and 101.325 [[Pascal (unit)|kPa]]
This constant is related to the Avogadro constant by the relation [[Gas constant|''R'']] = ''N''<sub>A</sub>[[Boltzmann constant|''k''<sub>B</sub>]], hence
::''N''<sub>A</sub> = ''n''<sub>0</sub>''p''/''k''<sub>B</sub>''T''
The connection with Loschmidt is the explanation for the symbol ''L'', often used instead of ''N''<sub>A</sub> to refer to the Avogadro constant.

Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions of the [[mole (unit)|mole]], and hence of the Avagadro number (as it was known at the time), based on 16 grams of oxygen: physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 as the basis ended this dispute and had other advantages.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html|accessdate=2006-12-28|title=Unit of amount of substance (mole)|last=[[Bureau international des poids et mesures]]|date=March 2006}}</ref>

At this time, the Avogadro number was defined as the number of atoms in 12 grams of carbon-12, that is as a [[dimensionless quantity]], while a mole was defined as one Avogadro number of atoms, molecules or other entities. When the mole entered the [[International System of Units|International System of Units (SI)]] in 1971 as the base unit of amount of substance, the definitions were interchanged: what had previously been a number became a [[physical constant]] with the unit of reciprocal moles (mol<sup>−1</sup>).

The genitive form "Avogardo's constant (number)" is often used but not recommended, particularly as Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant himself.<ref>Had Avogadro been able to measure the Avogadro constant, he would have determined a value approximately half of that used today, as relative atomic masses at that time were determined on a scale of [[Hydrogen|H<sub>2</sub>]] = 1. In fact Avogadro's proposal, which implied atoms and molecules had a physical, measurable existence, was not widely accepted until after his death. ''See'' [[Amadeo Avogadro]].</ref>

== Application ==
The Avogadro constant can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 g/mol, so ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms. The Avogadro constant also enters into the definition of the [[Unified atomic mass unit]], u:
::1 [[Unified atomic mass unit|u]] = (1/''N''<sub>A</sub>) [[Gram|g]] = (1.660 538 86 ± 0.000 000 28)×10<sup>−24</sup> [[Gram|g]]

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, Avogadro's number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between the [[atomic unit|atomic scale]] and the macroscopic scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>: [[Gas constant|''R'']] = [[Boltzmann constant|''k''<sub>B</sub>]]''N''<sub>A</sub> = (8.314 472 ± 0.000 015) [[Joule (unit)|J]][[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]][[Kelvin (unit)|K<sup>−1</sup>]]
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] ''e'': [[Faraday constant|''F'']] = ''N''<sub>A</sub>[[Elementary charge|''e'']] = (96 485.3383 ± 0.0083) [[Coulomb (unit)|C]][[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]]

== Measurement of the Avogadro constant ==
A number of methods can be used to measure the Avogadro constant. One modern method is to calculate the Avogadro constant from the density (ρ) of a crystal, the relative atomic mass (''M''), and the unit cell length (''a'') determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for [[silicon]] have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of the Avogadro constant:
::''N''<sub>A</sub> = 4''M''/''a''<sup>3</sup>ρ (for silicon)

The '''Avogadro constant''' (symbols: ''L'', ''N''<sub>A</sub>), also called the '''Avogadro number''' and, in German scientific literature, sometimes also known as the [[Loschmidt constant|Loschmidt constant/number]], is formally defined to be the number of "entities" in one [[mole (unit)|mole]],<ref>{{cite book|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Physicochemical Symbols Terminology and Units|title=Quantities, Units and Symbols in Physical Chemistry (2nd Edition)|publisher=Blackwell Scientific Publications|location=Oxford|date=1993
|url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/books/gbook/green_book_2ed.pdf|id=ISBN 0-632-03583-8|accessdate=2006-12-28}} {{cite journal|title=Glossary of Terms in Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry (IUPAC-IFCC Recommendations 1996)| |url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1996/pdf/6804x0957.pdf|journal=[[Pure and Applied Chemistry|Pure Appl. Chem.]]|volume=68|pages=957–1000|year=1996|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry|coauthors=[[International Federation of Clinical Chemistry]] Committee on Quantities and Units|accesdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> that is the number of [[carbon-12]] [[atom]]s in 12 [[gram]]s (0.012 [[Kilogram|kg]]) of unbound carbon-12 in its rest-energy electronic state. The current best estimate of this number is (6.022 1415 ± 0.000 0010)×10<sup>23</sup> [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]].<ref>[[Committee on Data for Science and Technology|CODATA]] [http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?na|search_for=abbr_in! (2002)].</ref>

== History and terminology ==
The '''Avogadro constant''' is named after the early [[nineteenth century]] [[Italy|Italian]] scientist [[Amedeo Avogadro]], who is credited with being the first to realize that the volume of a gas (strictly, of an [[ideal gas]]) is proportional to the number of atoms or [[molecule]]s.
It appears that [[Jean Baptiste Perrin]] was the first to name it.

Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant: the numerical value was first estimated by the [[Austria]]n physicist [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[kinetic theory of gases]].<ref>{{cite journal|title=Joseph Loschmidt, Physicist and Chemist|last=Bader|first=Alfred|coauthors=Parker, Leonard|journal=Physics Today Online|issue=March 2001|url=http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html|accessdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> In German-speaking countries, the constant may still be referred to as the ''[[Loschmidt constant]]'' or ''Loschmidt's number'': however this name is more correctly reserved for the number of particles in a given volume of an [[ideal gas]] (symbol:''n''<sub>0</sub>):<ref>{{cite web|url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?n0|search_for=physchem_in!|title=Fundamental physical constants: Physico-chemical constants|accessdate=2006-12-28|last=[[National Institute of Standards and Technology]]|date=February 2006}}</ref>
::''n''<sub>0</sub> = ''RT''/''p'' = (2.686 7773 ± 0.000 0047)×10<sup>25</sup> m<sup>−3</sup> at 273.15 [[Kelvin (unit)|K]] and 101.325 [[Pascal (unit)|kPa]]
This constant is related to the Avogadro constant by the relation [[Gas constant|''R'']] = ''N''<sub>A</sub>[[Boltzmann constant|''k''<sub>B</sub>]], hence
::''N''<sub>A</sub> = ''n''<sub>0</sub>''p''/''k''<sub>B</sub>''T''
The connection with Loschmidt is the explanation for the symbol ''L'', often used instead of ''N''<sub>A</sub> to refer to the Avogadro constant.

Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions of the [[mole (unit)|mole]], and hence of the Avogadro number (as it was known at the time), based on 16 grams of oxygen: physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 as the basis ended this dispute and had other advantages.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html|accessdate=2006-12-28|title=Unit of amount of substance (mole)|last=[[Bureau international des poids et mesures]]|date=March 2006}}</ref>

At this time, the Avogadro number was defined as the number of atoms in 12 grams of carbon-12, that is as a [[dimensionless quantity]], while a mole was defined as one Avogadro number of atoms, molecules or other entities. When the mole entered the [[International System of Units|International System of Units (SI)]] in 1971 as the base unit of amount of substance, the definitions were interchanged: what had previously been a number became a [[physical constant]] with the unit of reciprocal moles (mol<sup>−1</sup>).

The genitive form "Avogadro's constant (number)" is often used but not recommended, particularly as Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant himself.<ref>Had Avogadro been able to measure the Avogadro constant, he would have determined a value approximately half of that used today, as relative atomic masses at that time were determined on a scale of [[Hydrogen|H<sub>2</sub>]] = 1. In fact Avogadro's proposal, which implied atoms and molecules had a physical, measurable existence, was not widely accepted until after his death. ''See'' [[Amadeo Avogadro]].</ref>

== Application ==
The Avogadro constant can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 g/mol, so ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms. The Avogadro constant also enters into the definition of the [[unified atomic mass unit]], u:
:<math>1 \ u = \frac{1}{N_A} \ g = (1.660 \, 538\, 86 \pm 0.000\, 000\, 28) 10^{-24} \ g</math>

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, Avogadro's number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between the [[atomic unit|atomic scale]] and the macroscopic scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>: [[Gas constant|''R'']] = [[Boltzmann constant|''k''<sub>B</sub>]]''N''<sub>A</sub> = (8.314 472 ± 0.000 015) [[Joule (unit)|J]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]] [[Kelvin (unit)|K<sup>−1</sup>]]
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] ''e'': [[Faraday constant|''F'']] = ''N''<sub>A</sub>[[Elementary charge|''e'']] = (96 485.3383 ± 0.0083) [[Coulomb (unit)|C]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]]

== Measurement of the Avogadro constant ==
A number of methods can be used to measure the Avogadro constant. One modern method is to calculate the Avogadro constant from the density (ρ) of a crystal, the relative atomic mass (''M''), and the unit cell length (''a'') determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for [[silicon]] have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of the Avogadro constant:
::''N''<sub>A</sub> = 4''M''/''a''<sup>3</sup>ρ (for silicon)

::''N''<sub>A</sub> = ''n''<sub>0</sub>''p''/''k''<sub>B</sub>''T''
The connection with Loschmidt is the explanation for the symbol ''L'', often used instead of ''N''<sub>A</sub> to refer to the Avogadro constant.

Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions of the [[mole (unit)|mole]], and hence of the Avogadro number (as it was known at the time), based on 16 grams of oxygen: physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 as the basis ended this dispute and had other advantages.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html|accessdate=2006-12-28|title=Unit of amount of substance (mole)|last=[[Bureau international des poids et mesures]]|date=March 2006}}</ref>

At this time, the Avogadro number was defined as the number of atoms in 12 grams of carbon-12, that is as a [[dimensionless quantity]], while a mole was defined as one Avogadro number of atoms, molecules or other entities. When the mole entered the [[International System of Units|International System of Units (SI)]] in 1971 as the base unit of amount of substance, the definitions were interchanged: what had previously been a number became a [[physical constant]] with the unit of reciprocal moles (mol<sup>−1</sup>).

The genitive form "Avogadro's constant (number)" is often used but not recommended, particularly as Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant himself.<ref>Had Avogadro been able to measure the Avogadro constant, he would have determined a value approximately half of that used today, as relative atomic masses at that time were determined on a scale of [[Hydrogen|H<sub>2</sub>]] = 1. In fact Avogadro's proposal, which implied atoms and molecules had a physical, measurable existence, was not widely accepted until after his death. ''See'' [[Amadeo Avogadro]].</ref>

== Application ==
The Avogadro constant can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 g/mol, so ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms. The Avogadro constant also enters into the definition of the [[unified atomic mass unit]], u:
:<math>1 \ u = \frac{1}{N_A} \ g = (1.660 \, 538\, 86 \pm 0.000\, 000\, 28) 10^{-24} \ g</math>

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, Avogadro's number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between the [[atomic unit|atomic scale]] and the macroscopic scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>: [[Gas constant|''R'']] = [[Boltzmann constant|''k''<sub>B</sub>]]''N''<sub>A</sub> = (8.314 472 ± 0.000 015) [[Joule (unit)|J]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]] [[Kelvin (unit)|K<sup>−1</sup>]]
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] ''e'': [[Faraday constant|''F'']] = ''N''<sub>A</sub>[[Elementary charge|''e'']] = (96 485.3383 ± 0.0083) [[Coulomb (unit)|C]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]]

== Measurement of the Avogadro constant ==
A number of methods can be used to measure the Avogadro constant. One modern method is to calculate the Avogadro constant from the density (ρ) of a crystal, the relative atomic mass (''M''), and the unit cell length (''a'') determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for [[silicon]] have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of the Avogadro constant:
::''N''<sub>A</sub> = 4''M''/''a''<sup>3</sup>ρ (for silicon)

The '''Avogadro constant'''  
(symbols: ''L'', ''N''<sub>A</sub>), also called the '''Avogadro number''' and, in German scientific literature, sometimes also known as the [[Loschmidt constant|Loschmidt constant/number]], is formally defined to be the number of "entities" in one [[mole (unit)|mole]],<ref>{{cite book|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Physicochemical Symbols Terminology and Units|title=Quantities, Units and Symbols in Physical Chemistry (2nd Edition)|publisher=Blackwell Scientific Publications|location=Oxford|date=1993
|url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/books/gbook/green_book_2ed.pdf|id=ISBN 0-632-03583-8|accessdate=2006-12-28}} {{cite journal|title=Glossary of Terms in Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry (IUPAC-IFCC Recommendations 1996)| |url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1996/pdf/6804x0957.pdf|journal=[[Pure and Applied Chemistry|Pure Appl. Chem.]]|volume=68|pages=957–1000|year=1996|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry|coauthors=[[International Federation of Clinical Chemistry]] Committee on Quantities and Units|accesdate=2006-12-28}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1992/pdf/6410x1535.pdf|title=Atomic Weight: The Name, Its History, Definition and Units|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Atomic Weights and Isotopic Abundances|journal=[[Pure and Applied Chemistry|Pure Appl. Chem.]]|year=1992|volume=64|pages=1535–43|accessdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> that is the number of [[carbon-12]] [[atom]]s in 12 [[gram]]s (0.012 [[Kilogram|kg]]) of unbound carbon-12 in its rest-energy electronic state. The current best estimate of this number is <ref>[[Committee on Data for Science and Technology|CODATA]] [http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?na|search_for=abbr_in! (2006)].</ref>:


:<math>N_A = (6.022 \, 141 \, 79\pm 0.000 \, 000 \, 30)10^{23} \mbox{ mole}^{-1} \,</math>


== History and terminology ==
The '''Avogadro constant''' is named after the early [[nineteenth century]] [[Italy|Italian]] scientist [[Amedeo Avogadro]], who is credited with being the first to realize that the volume of a gas (strictly, of an [[ideal gas]]) is proportional to the number of atoms or [[molecule]]s.
The French chemist [[Jean Baptiste Perrin]] in 1909 proposed naming the constant in honor of Avogadro. American chemistry textbooks picked it up in the 1930's followed by highschool textbooks starting in the 1950s <ref>''How and When Did Avogadro's Name become Associated with Avogadro's Number?''  Jensen, William B. J. Chem. Educ. '''2007''' 84 223. [http://jchemed.chem.wisc.edu/Journal/Issues/2007/Feb/abs223.html Link]</ref>.
 

Avogadro never attempted to learn kung fu!!! measure the constant: the numerical value was first estimated by the [[Austria]]n physicist [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[kinetic theory of gases]].<ref>{{cite journal|title=Joseph Loschmidt, Physicist and Chemist|last=Bader|first=Alfred|coauthors=Parker, Leonard|journal=Physics Today Online|issue=March 2001|url=http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html|accessdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> In German-speaking countries, the constant may still be referred to as the ''[[Loschmidt constant]]'' or ''Loschmidt's number'': however this name is more correctly reserved for the number of particles in a given volume of an [[ideal gas]] (symbol:''n''<sub>0</sub>):<ref>{{cite web|url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?n0|search_for=physchem_in!|title=Fundamental physical constants: Physico-chemical constants|accessdate=2006-12-28|last=[[National Institute of Standards and Technology]]|date=February 2006}}</ref>

:<math>n_\circ = \frac{RT}{p}  \,</math>

equal to  (2.686 7773 ± 0.000 0047)×10<sup>25</sup> m<sup>−3</sup> at 273.15 [[Kelvin (unit)|K]] and 101.325 [[Pascal (unit)|kPa]] with R the [[gas constant]], T the [[temperature]] and p the [[pressure]].

This constant is related to the Avogadro constant by the relation:

:<math> R = N_Ak_B  \,</math>

with k<sub>B</sub> the [[Boltzmann constant]] hence

:<math>N_A = \frac{n_\circ p}{k_B T}  \,</math>

The connection with Loschmidt is the explanation for the symbol ''L'', often used instead of ''N''<sub>A</sub> to refer to the Avogadro constant.

Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions of the [[mole (unit)|mole]], and hence of the Avogadro number (as it was known at the time), based on 16 grams of oxygen: physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 as the basis ended this dispute and had other advantages.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html|accessdate=2006-12-28|title=Unit of amount of substance (mole)|last=[[Bureau international des poids et mesures]]|date=March 2006}}</ref>

At this time, the Avogadro number was defined as the number of atoms in 12 grams of carbon-12, that is as a [[dimensionless quantity]], while a mole was defined as one Avogadro number of atoms, molecules or other entities. When the mole entered the [[International System of Units|International System of Units (SI)]] in 1971 as the base unit of amount of substance, the definitions were interchanged: what had previously been a number became a [[physical constant]] with the unit of reciprocal moles (mol<sup>−1</sup>).

The genitive form "Avogadro's constant (number)" is often used but not recommended, particularly as Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant himself.<ref>Had Avogadro been able to measure the Avogadro constant, he would have determined a value approximately half of that used today, as relative atomic masses at that time were determined on a scale of [[Hydrogen|H<sub>2</sub>]] = 1. In fact Avogadro's proposal, which implied atoms and molecules had a physical, measurable existence, was not widely accepted until after his death. ''See'' [[Amadeo Avogadro]].</ref>

== Application ==
The Avogadro constant can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 g/mol, so ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms. The Avogadro constant also enters into the definition of the [[unified atomic mass unit]], u:
:<math>1 \ u = \frac{1}{N_A} \ g = (1.660 \, 538\, 86 \pm 0.000\, 000\, 28) 10^{-24} \ g</math>

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, the Avogadro number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between the [[atomic unit|atomic scale]] and the macroscopic scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>:

:<math> R = k_BN_A =   8.314 \, 472 \, \pm \, 0.000 \, 015 \, Jmole^{-1}K^{-1}\,</math>

:in [[Joule (unit)|J]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]] [[Kelvin (unit)|K<sup>−1</sup>]]
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] ''e'': 

:<math> F = N_Ae =   96 \, 485.3383 \, \pm \,0.0083 \,\, Cmole^{-1} \,</math>

:in [[Coulomb (unit)|C]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]]

== Measurement of the Avogadro constant ==
A number of methods can be used to measure the Avogadro constant. One modern method is to calculate the Avogadro constant from the [[density]] (ρ) of a crystal, the relative atomic mass (''M''), and the unit cell length (''a'') determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for [[silicon]] have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of the Avogadro constant:

:<math> N_A  =  \frac{4M}{a^3\rho} \,</math>

:based on silicon.

The '''Avogadro constant'''  
(symbols: ''L'', ''N''<sub>A</sub>), also called the '''Avogadro number''' and, in German scientific literature, sometimes also known as the [[Loschmidt constant|Loschmidt constant/number]], is formally defined to be the number of "entities" in one [[mole (unit)|mole]],<ref>{{cite book|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Physicochemical Symbols Terminology and Units|title=Quantities, Units and Symbols in Physical Chemistry (2nd Edition)|publisher=Blackwell Scientific Publications|location=Oxford|date=1993
|url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/books/gbook/green_book_2ed.pdf|id=ISBN 0-632-03583-8|accessdate=2006-12-28}} {{cite journal|title=Glossary of Terms in Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry (IUPAC-IFCC Recommendations 1996)| |url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1996/pdf/6804x0957.pdf|journal=[[Pure and Applied Chemistry|Pure Appl. Chem.]]|volume=68|pages=957–1000|year=1996|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Quantities and Units in Clinical Chemistry|coauthors=[[International Federation of Clinical Chemistry]] Committee on Quantities and Units|accesdate=2006-12-28}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|url=http://www.iupac.org/publications/pac/1992/pdf/6410x1535.pdf|title=Atomic Weight: The Name, Its History, Definition and Units|last=[[International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry]] Commission on Atomic Weights and Isotopic Abundances|journal=[[Pure and Applied Chemistry|Pure Appl. Chem.]]|year=1992|volume=64|pages=1535–43|accessdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> that is the number of [[carbon-12]] [[atom]]s in 12 [[gram]]s (0.012 [[Kilogram|kg]]) of unbound carbon-12 in its rest-energy electronic state. The current best estimate of this number is <ref>[[Committee on Data for Science and Technology|CODATA]] [http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?na|search_for=abbr_in! (2006)].</ref>:

:<big><big><math>N_A = (6.022 \, 141 \, 79\pm 0.000 \, 000 \, 30)\,\times\,10^{23} \mbox{ mole}^{-1} \,</math></big></big>

== History and terminology ==
The '''Avogadro constant''' is named after the early Samuel Adekanye [[nineteenth century]] [[Italy|Italian]] scientist [[Amedeo Avogadro]], who is credited with being the first man to come from Swaziland and realize that the volume of a gas (strictly, of an [[ideal gas]]) is proportional to the number of atoms or [[molecule]]s. With help from Samuel Adekanyne's slave Russell Whitfield Barnes (6-3 250lbs.) he was able to complete his findings with ease because Whitfield could bench press 300lbs. and starts on the football team. 
. American chemistry textbooks picked it up in the 1930's followed by highschool textbooks starting in the 1950s.<ref>''How and When Did Avogadro's Name become Associated with Avogadro's Number?''  Jensen, William B. J. Chem. Educ. '''2007''' 84 223. [http://jchemed.chem.wisc.edu/Journal/Issues/2007/Feb/abs223.html Link]</ref>

Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant: the numerical value was first estimated by the [[Austria]]n physicist [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[kinetic theory of gases]].<ref>{{cite journal|title=Joseph Loschmidt, Physicist and Chemist|last=Bader|first=Alfred|coauthors=Parker, Leonard|journal=Physics Today Online|issue=March 2001|url=http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html|accessdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> In German-speaking countries, the constant may still be referred to as the ''[[Loschmidt constant]]'' or ''Loschmidt's number'': however this name is more correctly reserved for the number of particles in a given volume of an [[ideal gas]] (symbol:''n''<sub>0</sub>):<ref>{{cite web|url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?n0|search_for=physchem_in!|title=Fundamental physical constants: Physico-chemical constants|accessdate=2006-12-28|last=[[National Institute of Standards and Technology]]|date=February 2006}}</ref>

:<math>n_\circ = \frac{RT}{p}  \,</math>

equal to  (2.686 7773 ± 0.000 0047)×10<sup>25</sup> m<sup>−3</sup> at 273.15 [[Kelvin (unit)|K]] and 101.325 [[Pascal (unit)|kPa]] with R the [[gas constant]], T the [[temperature]] and p the [[pressure]].

This constant is related to the Avogadro constant by the relation:

:<math> R = N_Ak_B  \,</math>

with k<sub>B</sub> the [[Boltzmann constant]] hence

:<math>N_A = \frac{n_\circ p}{k_B T}  \,</math>

The connection with Loschmidt is the explanation for the symbol ''L'', often used instead of ''N''<sub>A</sub> to refer to the Avogadro constant.

Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions of the [[mole (unit)|mole]], and hence of the Avogadro number (as it was known at the time), based on 16 grams of oxygen: physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 as the basis ended this dispute and had other advantages.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html|accessdate=2006-12-28|title=Unit of amount of substance (mole)|last=[[Bureau international des poids et mesures]]|date=March 2006}}</ref>

At this time, the Avogadro number was defined as the number of atoms in 12 grams of carbon-12, that is as a [[dimensionless quantity]], while a mole was defined as one Avogadro number of atoms, molecules or other entities. When the mole entered the [[International System of Units|International System of Units (SI)]] in 1971 as the base unit of amount of substance, the definitions were interchanged: what had previously been a number became a [[physical constant]] with the unit of reciprocal moles (mol<sup>−1</sup>).

The genitive form "Avogadro's constant (number)" is often used but not recommended, particularly as Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant himself.<ref>Had Avogadro been able to measure the Avogadro constant, he would have determined a value approximately half of that used today, as relative atomic masses at that time were determined on a scale of [[Hydrogen|H<sub>2</sub>]] = 1. In fact Avogadro's proposal, which implied atoms and molecules had a physical, measurable existence, was not widely accepted until after his death. ''See'' [[Amadeo Avogadro]].</ref>

== Application ==
The Avogadro constant can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 g/mol, so ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms. The Avogadro constant also enters into the definition of the [[unified atomic mass unit]], u:
:<math>1 \ u = \frac{1}{N_A} \ g = (1.660 \, 538\, 86 \pm 0.000\, 000\, 28) 10^{-24} \ g</math>

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, the Avogadro number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between the [[atomic unit|atomic scale]] and the macroscopic scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>:

:<math> R = k_BN_A =   8.314 \, 472 \, \pm \, 0.000 \, 015 \, Jmole^{-1}K^{-1}\,</math>

:in [[Joule (unit)|J]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]] [[Kelvin (unit)|K<sup>−1</sup>]]
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] ''e'': 

:<math> F = N_Ae =   96 \, 485.3383 \, \pm \,0.0083 \,\, Cmole^{-1} \,</math>

:in [[Coulomb (unit)|C]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]]

== Measurement of the Avogadro constant ==
A number of methods can be used to measure the Avogadro constant. One modern method is to calculate the Avogadro constant from the [[density]] (ρ) of a crystal, the relative atomic mass (''M''), and the unit cell length (''a'') determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for [[silicon]] have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of the Avogadro constant:

:<math> N_A  =  \frac{4M}{a^3\rho} \,</math>

:based on silicon.

Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant: the numerical value was first estimated by the [[Austria]]n physicist [[Johann Josef Loschmidt]] in [[1865]] using the [[kinetic theory of gases]].<ref>{{cite journal|title=Joseph Loschmidt, Physicist and Chemist|last=Bader|first=Alfred|coauthors=Parker, Leonard|journal=Physics Today Online|issue=March 2001|url=http://www.physicstoday.org/pt/vol-54/iss-3/p45.html|accessdate=2006-12-28}}</ref> In German-speaking countries, the constant may still be referred to as the ''[[Loschmidt constant]]'' or ''Loschmidt's number'': However this name is more correctly reserved for the number of particles in a given volume of an [[ideal gas]] (symbol:''n''<sub>0</sub>):<ref>{{cite web|url=http://physics.nist.gov/cgi-bin/cuu/Value?n0|search_for=physchem_in!|title=Fundamental physical constants: Physico-chemical constants|accessdate=2006-12-28|last=[[National Institute of Standards and Technology]]|date=February 2006}}</ref>

:<math>n_\circ = \frac{RT}{p}  \,</math>

equal to  (2.686 7773 ± 0.000 0047)×10<sup>25</sup> m<sup>−3</sup> at 273.15 [[Kelvin (unit)|K]] and 101.325 [[Pascal (unit)|kPa]] with R the [[gas constant]], T the [[temperature]] and p the [[pressure]].

This constant is related to the Avogadro constant by the relation:

:<math> R = N_Ak_C  \,</math>

with k<sub>B</sub> the [[Boltzmann constant]] hence

:<math>N_A = \frac{n_\circ p}{k_B T}  \,</math>

The connection with Loschmidt is the explanation for the symbol ''L'', often used instead of ''N''<sub>A</sub> to refer to the Avogadro constant.

Before 1960, there were conflicting definitions of the [[mole (unit)|mole]], and hence of the Avogadro number (as it was known at the time), based on 16 grams of oxygen: physicists generally used oxygen-16 while chemists generally used the "naturally occurring" isotope ratio.  Switching to 12 grams of carbon-12 as the basis ended this dispute and had other advantages.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.bipm.fr/en/si/si_brochure/chapter2/2-1/mole.html|accessdate=2006-12-28|title=Unit of amount of substance (mole)|last=[[Bureau international des poids et mesures]]|date=March 2006}}</ref>

At this time, the Avogadro number was defined as the number of atoms in 12 grams of carbon-12, that is as a [[dimensionless quantity]], while a mole was defined as one Avogadro number of atoms, molecules or other entities. When the mole entered the [[International System of Units|International System of Units (SI)]] in 1971 as the base unit of amount of substance, the definitions were interchanged: what had previously been a number became a [[physical constant]] with the unit of reciprocal moles (mol<sup>−1</sup>).

The genitive form "Avogadro's constant (number)" is often used but not recommended, particularly as Avogadro never attempted to measure the constant himself.<ref>Had Avogadro been able to measure the Avogadro constant, he would have determined a value approximately half of that used today, as relative atomic masses at that time were determined on a scale of [[Hydrogen|H<sub>2</sub>]] = 1. In fact Avogadro's proposal, which implied atoms and molecules had a physical, measurable existence, was not widely accepted until after his death. ''See'' [[Amadeo Avogadro]].</ref>

== Application ==
The Avogadro constant can be applied to any substance. It corresponds to the number of atoms or molecules needed to make up a mass equal to the substance's [[atomic mass|atomic]] or [[molecular mass]], in grams. For example, the atomic mass of iron is 55.847 g/mol, so ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms (i.e. one mole of iron atoms) have a mass of 55.847 g. Conversely, 55.847 g of iron contains ''N''<sub>A</sub> iron atoms. The Avogadro constant also enters into the definition of the [[unified atomic mass unit]], u:
:<math>1 \ u = \frac{1}{N_A} \ g = (1.660 \, 538\, 86 \pm 0.000\, 000\, 28) 10^{-22} \ g</math>

== Additional physical relations ==
Because of its role as a scaling factor, the Avogadro number provides the link between a number of useful physical constants when moving between the [[atomic unit|atomic scale]] and the macroscopic scale. For example, it provides the relationship between:
* the [[gas constant]] ''R'' and the [[Boltzmann constant]] ''k''<sub>B</sub>:

:<math> R = k_BN_A =   8.314 \, 472 \, \pm \, 0.000 \, 015 \, Jmole^{-1}K^{-1}\,</math>

:in [[Joule (unit)|J]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]] [[Kelvin (unit)|K<sup>−1</sup>]]
* the [[Faraday constant]] ''F'' and the [[elementary charge]] ''e'': 

:<math> F = N_Ae =   96 \, 485.3383 \, \pm \,0.0083 \,\, Cmole^{-1} \,</math>

:in [[Coulomb (unit)|C]] [[mole (unit)|mol<sup>−1</sup>]]

== Measurement of the Avogadro constant ==
A number of methods can be used to measure the Avogadro constant. One modern method is to calculate the Avogadro constant from the [[density]] (ρ) of a crystal, the relative atomic mass (''M''), and the unit cell length (''a'') determined from [[x-ray crystallography]]. Very accurate values of these quantities for [[silicon]] have been measured at the [[National Institute of Standards and Technology]] (NIST) and used to obtain the value of the Avogadro constant:

:<math> N_A  =  \frac{4M}{a^3\rho} \,</math>

:based on silicon.
